data.title: French Manufacturing Sector Remains Fragile in October
data.description:
The HCOB Flash France Manufacturing PMI rose slightly to 48.3 in October 2025 from 48.2 in September, in line with market expectations, according to preliminary estimates.

Although this marks a two-month high, the figure still points to contraction, signaling sharper declines in production and new orders.

Sluggish client demand and ongoing domestic political uncertainty, together with weak global conditions, continued to weigh on exports.

In response, manufacturers cut output prices for a second consecutive month to stimulate demand, while input cost inflation eased to its slowest pace in nearly five years as energy and material prices stabilized.

Employment grew modestly, mainly through temporary hires, reflecting cautious workforce planning.

Business confidence slipped to its lowest since mid-year, with many firms expecting further production declines amid political instability and soft external demand.

data.title: Stressed market conditions in derivatives on HIAB (259/25)
data.description:
NASDAQ NordicProduct information

Stressed market conditions in derivatives on HIAB (259/25)

Nasdaq has decided to call for stressed market conditions in derivatives onHiab Oyj (HIAB) for four hours, after which time an assessment of marketconditions will be made to determine if stressed market conditions are stillvalid.

Stressed market conditions in derivatives are called for when the underlyingasset to a derivative experience high and short term intraday volatility orwhen other factors causes high uncertainty in the pricing of derivatives. Whenstressed market is called for it means that market makers are allowed to quoteprices with double the normal spread.

For contact details please see attached file.https://view.news.eu.nasdaq.com/view?id=b881486cd07125769cd22a7564c663012&lang=en&src=omxlink

data.title: German Manufacturing Remains in Contraction
data.description:
The HCOB Germany Manufacturing PMI ticked up to 49.6 in October 2025, compared to market expectations and September’s 49.5, signaling a continued mild contraction in the sector.

Output expanded for the eighth consecutive month, though at a slower pace, with the output index easing to a three-month low.

There was a marginal rise in new orders after the previous month’s dip, while backlogs continued to decline.

Employment fell for the seventeenth straight month, the sharpest drop since June, as manufacturers remained cautious amid supply chain challenges, particularly in semiconductors and machinery.

On prices, input costs continued to fall, albeit at a slower rate, while output prices increased for only the second time in two and a half years.

Business confidence weakened further, reflecting concerns over high costs, global competition, and persistent uncertainty in the industrial outlook.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0342 ET - The Canadian dollar faces further losses after President Trump said he was terminating trade negotiations with Canada, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. The Canadian dollar's falls are modest because there had been little to no progress in U.S.-Canada talks so far and the currency was already undervalued against the U.S. dollar, Pesole says. "But if anything, we think that this development slightly increases the chance of another Bank of Canada rate cut next [Wednesday]." It'll also be hard for the BOC to close the door on further rate cuts, he says. The U.S. dollar rises 0.2% to 1.4014 Canadian dollars and ING sees scope for it to reach 1.4100 in the near-term.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0317 ET - Oil prices fall slightly after rallying on additional U.S sanctions on Russia's energy sector. Prices fall with Brent crude down 0.6% to $65.60 a barrel and WTI is down 0.5% to $61.46 a barrel. Prices had rallied more than 5% Thursday. The U.S. sanctions come as markets have been concerned about a supply glut fueled by OPEC production ramp-ups and cooling demand, ANZ analysts write. The impact of the sanctions will be determined by whether Russia can find an alternative buyer for the crude it has been sending to India, they write. China has been buying Russian crude but might be reluctant to boost purchases further, they add. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0259 ET - Bitcoin rises after the White House confirmed that President Trump would meet with Chinese leader Xi Jinping next week in South Korea. The two leaders will meet on October 30 on the sidelines of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation Summit. Trump said later Thursday that he thinks "we are going to come out very well and everyone's going to be very happy." The news raises hopes for a de-escalation in tensions. Trump's threat earlier this month of fresh tariffs on China sparked a selloff in cryptocurrencies. Bitcoin rises 1.6% to $111,390, LSEG data show. It has recovered from last Friday's near four-month low of $103,659 but remains well below the Oct.6 record high of $126,223. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

2303 ET - The Singapore dollar weakens slightly against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session amid concerns over U.S.-China trade ties. The U.S. Trade Representative's office is preparing to open a new investigation into how the Chinese government has upheld its end of the so-called Phase One trade deal that it signed during President Trump's first term, the WSJ reported, citing people familiar with the agency's planning. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, along with Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, are scheduled to meet China Vice Premier He Lifeng in Malaysia today. All eyes are on this meeting, Maybank analysts say in a FX Research & Strategy report. USD/SGD is 0.1% higher at 1.2995, FactSet data show. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1606 ET - Ford Motor said its underlying business is performing at the high end of the guidance range it shared in February, but unexpected headwinds are bogging it down. Ford in February said it expected to post $7 billion to $8.5 billion in adjusted pretax earnings. Now, it anticipates that figure to be $6 billion to $6.5 billion, it said. The lower numbers are related to a $1 billion net headwind from tariffs, as well as added costs it expects from a fire earlier this month at its aluminum supplier's plant. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1419 ET - CBOT grain futures are higher after the White House says President Trump is scheduled to meet with Chinese leader Xi Jinping next Thursday in a bilateral meeting that will be part of a multi-country trip to Asia. It's a positive sign for grain traders, who anticipate that a trade deal could be nearing with a potential restart of China purchasing U.S. soybean exports. But some remain skeptical that the meeting will yield positive news. "U.S./China tensions appear to be worsening--not improving," says Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note. CBOT corn is up 1.2%, soybeans rise 0.8%, and wheat climbs 1.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1033 ET - U.S. sanctions on Russia's largest oil companies mark "a step change" in efforts to pressure Moscow on the war in Ukraine and there's a good chance it will make it materially more difficult for Russia to bring oil to market, says Anthony Rapa, co-chair of Blank Rome's international trade practice. "Of the cards the U.S. had left to play this was a big one. From U.S. perspective it's going to come down to how do the major importers of Russian oil react to this," he says. While the asset freeze is probably not that big of a deal for Rosneft and Lukoil, the secondary sanctions and prospects of sanctions on any banks or companies that provide support to them or their affiliates will weigh more, he adds. "Not everybody will be able to wave that off." Oil markets are reacting to the sanctions with WTI up 5.2% at $61.56 a barrel and Brent 4.9% higher at $65.64 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1019 ET - The foreign exchange market is currently showing indifference to fresh U.S. tariff threats, Commerzbank's Michael Pfister says in a note. This could be because the market has become desensitized to tariffs, he says. Another possibility is that the market knows where it stands and has priced the tariffs accordingly. Moreover, U.S. inflation expectations remain high due to tariffs and this means it will be difficult for the market to price in further Federal Reserve interest rate cuts. Rate cut expectations in other countries have also stabilized. Meanwhile, only minor effects are evident in trade data thus far. This doesn't mean tariffs aren't having an impact. "It may just take longer for this impact to become apparent." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0935 ET - Canadian businesses are worried about the fate of the North American trade pact and may expect to have to pay for U.S. market access. KPMG's annual federal budget survey of 501 Canadian business leaders finds more than nine in 10 believe the greatest risk to Canada's economic future is a negative outcome in renegotiating the free trade agreement with the U.S. And 82% of leaders believe that U.S. tariffs, including on Canada, are here to stay. Still, four in five business leaders say they would support a bilateral agreement with the U.S. only and 73% of respondents say they can't afford the added costs of exploring new markets. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0929 ET - CBOT grain futures are generally flat, with traders focused on whether there's confirmation of an upcoming meeting between President Trump and Chinese President Xi. "Weekend trade negotiations are high stakes with the U.S. threatening to limit software exports to China," says AgResource in a note. For grains, the resumption of U.S. soybean export purchasing by China is the most-important topic. Soybean futures are up 0.3%, while corn and wheat are unchanged. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0759 ET - The cost of insuring euro credit against default stays steady as investors exercise caution amid geopolitical and trade uncertainty. The U.S.-China trade conflict, the ongoing U.S. government shutdown and uncertainty ahead of Friday's macroeconomic data releases are causing investors to trade cautiously. Investors await U.S. inflation data, U.K. and eurozone interim purchasing managers' surveys and U.K. retail sales data, all due Friday. The iTraxx Europe Crossover index of euro junk bond credit default swaps and the iTraxx Europe Main index of euro investment-grade CDS remain steady at 270 basis points and 56bps, respectively, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0615 ET - The cost of insuring Chinese government bonds against default rises as the U.S.-China trade conflict weighs on market sentiment. "The Trump administration's proposal to impose new restrictions on software exports to China reignited concerns of further trade disputes," Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly says in a note. The Chinese 5-year sovereign credit default swap spreads rise 1 basis point to 45 bps in Thursday morning trade, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Saab's Guidance Upgrade Offsets Mixed Results — Market Talk
data.description: Saab's mixed third-quarter results are offset by upgraded full-year guidance, Morgan Stanley writes. The company reported softer order intake momentum compared to previous quarters at 20.9 billion Swedish kronor, which is 15% below consensus. However, the U.S. bank says momentum is likely to be back during the fourth quarter, with orders valued at over 16 billion kronor already closed. Sales in the quarter were 2% above consensus while profitability also beat views, and upgraded guidance implies 1%-5% upside to consensus expectations. As a result, Morgan Stanley believes the market will welcome the second sales guidance upgrade this year and will focus now on order momentum from the fourth quarter and a potential update of the midterm guidance, likely next year. Shares rise 4.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: German Private Sector Growth Highest Since 2023
data.description:
The HCOB Flash Germany Composite PMI increased to 53.8 in October 2025 from 52 in September, beating forecasts of 51.6.

The reading pointed to the strongest growth in private sector activity since May 2023, mostly due to a solid increase in services business activity (54.5, also the highest for almost two-and-a-half years vs 51.5) while the manufacturing sector approached stagnation (49.6 vs 49.5).

The upturn in output was supported by a renewed expansion in intakes of new work, while employment came close to stabilising as backlogs showed their first rise since mid-2022.

With growth strengthening and demand improving, inflationary pressures rose.

There were faster increases in both input costs and average charges for goods and services, with the respective rates of inflation reaching the quickest for eight months.

Meanwhile, there was a broad-based easing of businesses expectations for the year ahead, signalling the economic situation remains fragile.

data.title: Iron ore slips on falling Chinese demand
data.description:
Iron ore futures prices dipped on Friday, snapping a three-session winning streak, as demand in top consumer China softened, hit by shrinking steel margins.

The most-traded January iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) closed daytime trade 0.58% lower at 771 yuan ($108.24) a metric ton, posting a 0.1% weekly fall.

By 0711 GMT, the benchmark November iron ore (SZZFX5) on the Singapore Exchange was 0.57% lower at $104.05 a ton, an increase of 0.1% so far this week.

Lower hot metal output, a gauge of iron ore demand, dragged down prices of the key steelmaking ingredient, said Ying Cao, a Beijing-based analyst at broker SDIC Futures.

Average daily hot metal output slipped for a fourth straight week by 0.4% from the prior week to the lowest since September 5 at 2.4 million tons in the week as of October 23, data from consultancy Mysteel showed.

"We expect hot metal output to fall further in the coming weeks as stronger coal prices have squeezed steel margins, forcing some mills to trim output," said Cao.

Coking coal NYMEX:ACT1! and coke (DCJcv1), other steelmaking ingredients, extended gains, up 1.42% and 1.53%, respectively, bolstered by falling supply caused by halts of mining operations in some coal production hubs, analysts at GF Futures said.

That said, hopes of an easing trader tension between the world's two largest economies helped limit price loss.

Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng is set to meet U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Trade Representative Jamieson Greer from Friday, in a bid to ease an unexpected flare-up in tension ahead of a meeting between U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping.

Steel benchmarks on the Shanghai Futures Exchange moved sideways. Rebar shed 0.75%, wire rod (SWRcv1) lost 0.18% while hot-rolled coil ticked up 0.03% and stainless steel gained 0.71%.

($1 = 7.1230 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Accor Shares Rise After Profit Guidance Upgrade, Buyback
data.description:
By Maitane Sardon

Shares in Accor rose after the French hotel group lifted its profit outlook for the year and said it plans to buy back 100 million euros ($116.2 million) of shares in the fourth quarter.

In early European trade Friday, shares were up 5% at 44.30 euros.

The operator of Novotel and Ibis brands said it expects to grow earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization by 11% to 12% at constant currency rates, compared with previous estimates of between 9% and 10%.

The company said revenue rose 0.8% on-year in the third quarter to 1.37 billion euros. Analysts had forecast a 0.9% increase.

Performance was in line with expectations, Jefferies analysts said in a research note. The combination of positive comment about the current trading, the guidance upgrade, the share buyback, the potential listing of Ennismore and reassuring comments on the disposal of Essendi should be taken well, the analysts said.

Write to Maitane Sardon at maitane.sardon@wsj.com

data.title: Top banker says Russia needs millions of skilled migrants to boost growth
data.description:
Russia will need to attract millions of skilled migrants to achieve an economic growth rate of at least 3.2% and sustain its economy, the country's top banker and longtime ally of President Vladimir Putin said on Thursday.

German Gref, CEO of Russia's largest lender Sberbank, told members of the State Council on Demographic and Family Policy that higher growth could only be achieved by either increasing productivity or expanding the workforce.

"Without economic growth, there will be nothing. We won't be able to solve social problems, or any others. We must grow at a rate no lower than the global average. That means at least 3.2% annually until 2030," Gref said.

The country has faced acute labour shortages across multiple sectors since the start of the war in Ukraine, as hundreds of thousands joined the military. Generous payouts to army volunteers have also triggered a wage growth spiral.

Sberbank expects Russia’s economic growth to plunge to 0.8% in 2025, down from 4.3% last year. The central bank is expected to decide on its key interest rate on Friday, with economists evenly split on the outcome.

Gref argued that with high interest rates and limited investment capital due to Western sanctions making productivity gains difficult, the only viable path forward was to attract skilled labour from abroad.

Migration remains a politically sensitive issue in Russia, and public calls to increase migrant numbers are rare.

Russia has relied on foreign workers, mainly from ex-Soviet republics in Central Asia, in sectors such as construction and municipal services. Migration has been restricted since a deadly attack at a Moscow concert hall in 2024.

"Unfortunately, we are engaging in negative selection. We mostly attract very low-skilled labour, while we ourselves are losing highly qualified specialists,” Gref said. Many professionals fled Russia after the war began.

He urged the government to actively seek qualified professionals from abroad and create incentives for foreign students to remain in Russia after graduation.

"It is a matter of national security and the survival of the country," he said.

data.title: India stocks, rupee, bonds swaps, call at 1:05 p.m. IST
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex fell 0.34% to 84,266, while the broader NSE Nifty 50 was down 0.3% at 25,809, on profit booking after jumping 3% in the last six sessions.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee rose 0.1% versus the U.S. dollar to 87.7525, supported by likely inflows and the lingering impact of the Reserve Bank of India's persistent dollar offers near the 88 level.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN063335G=CC) was quoted at 98.52 rupees, with the yield up marginally at 6.5410%, as sentiment remains cautious after news of a potential U.S.-India trade deal that tempered rate cut expectations.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was down nearly 2 basis points at 5.4475%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) dropped 2 bps to 5.6450%.

CALL MONEY:

data.title: September Inflation Report: What You Need to Know — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Key points to know ahead of this morning's consumer-price index:

  • Prices likely rose 3.1% in September from a year earlier, according to economists polled by The Wall Street Journal.
  • Core CPI also likely rose at a 3.1% rate, according to the economists. This measure excludes volatile food and energy prices.
  • The headline inflation rate stood at 2.9% in August, with core inflation at 3.1%.
  • Economists expect September's monthly inflation to be 0.4%, or 0.3% excluding food and energy. Both rates would be the same as the August-July comparison.
  • The Labor Department report is due at 8:30 a.m. ET. It was delayed nine days because of the government shutdown.
  • Inflation data plays a big role in Federal Reserve interest-rate decisions, the next of which comes next Wednesday.
  • Tariff effects will be closely watched, including in today's CPI report.
  • Tariffs have helped spur a recent uptick in inflation, but price pressures haven't sped up as dramatically as many experts predicted. Debate continues about how much tariffs will continue to affect inflation.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Bajaj Healthcare reports revenue growth, driven by export performance
data.description:
Bajaj Healthcare Ltd. (Mumbai) announced its financial performance for the fiscal second quarter ended Sept. 30, reporting an 11% year-over-year growth in revenue, to 1.4 billion Indian rupees (16.8 million), as per Chemweek.

The company also achieved a profit of 111.1 million rupees, reflecting a 17.4% increase compared with the same period last year. Its EBITDA stood at 286 million rupees, marking a 5.4% year-over-year rise.The company’s active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) domestic business unit, however, faced challenges, reporting a 5.4% year-over-year decline in revenue to 762.8 million rupees. In contrast, the API exports division experienced growth, with revenues rising by 67% year over year to 487.3 million rupees.The formulations unit reported revenue of 229.1 million rupees, down 1.5% from the previous year. Despite the mixed results within its divisions, Bajaj Healthcare attributed its overall improvement in margins and profitability primarily to strong growth in exports.

The company acknowledged ongoing pricing pressures in the domestic API segment, but remains committed to pursuing opportunities in high-margin products to ensure long-term margin stability.

mrchub.com

data.title: Conapto expands Stockholm 4 South Data Center with 8 MW extension to meet growing AI and digitalization needs
data.description:
Conapto Holding AB

Stockholm, Sweden - 24 October 2025 - Conapto today announced a major expansion of its Stockholm 4 South data center, increasing its capacity with an additional 8 MW of power from energy company Ellevio. With this increase, the site will scale from 20 MW to 28 MW, strengthening Conapto's position as a leading provider of sustainable colocation in Stockholm, Sweden.

"Digitalization and AI are changing the way data is used around the world, and this expansion ensures our customers have the reliable and sustainable infrastructure to grow," said Håkan Björklund, CEO of Conapto. "Adding 8 MW at Stockholm 4 South is about more than capacity. It's about ensuring resilience, efficiency and climate-smart operations for the future."

The new power capacity will be ready starting January 1, 2026, giving customers the infrastructure they need to handle the rapidly accelerating demands of digital transformation, cloud growth and AI-driven applications.

"Expanding the capacity of Conapto's data center is not just a technical step; it's a critical aspect of securing a sustainable future. The AI innovations and digital services shaping our lives depend on the electrified infrastructure that both Conapto and Ellevio deliver. This expansion marks a milestone in our shared mission," said Tomas Brunzell, Ellevio Asset Owner.

Conapto's Stockholm 4 South facility is strategically located with access to robust connectivity and Sweden's renewable energy resources, making it an attractive hub for international enterprises, hyperscalers and AI-driven organizations. The expansion aligns with Conapto's sustainability strategy, which includes 100% renewable energy, heat reuse to district heating, grid support to help stabilize the national grid and strong commitments to lowering customers' environmental footprints.

This latest investment comes at a time when Sweden is establishing itself as one of the most sustainable and future-ready data center markets in Europe.

About Conapto

Conapto offers scalable, secure and sustainable data center colocation for physical hardware as well as market-leading access to communication and connectivity to public cloud services. Conapto has four data centers and its headquarters in Stockholm. Learn more at www.conapto.com.

About Ellevio

Ellevio is one of Sweden's leading energy companies, enabling the transition to a low carbon economy. We distribute electricity to almost one million customers and provide energy solutions across Sweden. We enable the electrification of industry and transportation through a modern, reliable grid and major investments in the energy system. Ellevio has about 800 employees and, together with partners, provides work for more than 4,000 people. The company is owned by pension fund managers AMF, Folksam, OMERS Infrastructure and the Third Swedish National Pension Fund (AP3).

Media Contact:

Anna Ekman

0734292295

Anna.ekman@conapto.se

data.title: Gold Set for Weekly Loss as Prices Slide — Market Talk
data.description:
0740 GMT - Gold prices fall further after a sharp correction earlier this week. In early trade, futures in New York are down 0.9% to $4,108.50 a troy ounce. The metal is heading for a weekly loss after a sell-off following gold hitting an all time high on Monday, MUFG's Soojin Kim writes. The fall was triggered by profit taking and coincided with largest single-day outflow from gold-backed exchange-traded funds in five months, Kim writes. Investor attention is now turning to the upcoming U.S. inflation data for clues on future interest cuts, she adds.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0740 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds fall, along with their eurozone peers, as expectations of a trade deal between the U.S. and China ease investor concerns. Gilt yields also remain lower after below-forecast U.K. inflation data earlier this week. They show little reaction to U.K. retail sales data for September which was better than expected, indicating that consumer spending remains strong. The 10-year gilt yield falls 1.1 basis points to last trade at 4.404%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0732 GMT - French government bonds, or OATs, and the euro are little changed after weaker-than-expected flash estimate purchasing managers' surveys for France. The composite PMI came in at 46.8 for October, below analyst expectations of 48.2 in the Wall Street Journal's poll, and also below September's print of 48.1. The 10-year OAT yield trades at 3.378%, up 0.1 basis point both before and after the data release, according to Tradeweb. The euro falls 0.1% to $1.1607, little changed from levels beforehand. Investors also await Moody's Ratings' review of France after the market close, with the prospect of yet another ratings downgrade. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0723 GMT - NetLink NBN Trust is likely a major beneficiary of the large fall in Singapore's risk-free rate, DBS Group Research's Sachin Mittal says in a research report. This rate, represented by the 10-year government bond yield, has dropped 70 bps over the past six months to 1.8%. This is particularly positive for a regulated business like Netlink NBN Trust, which trades at a yield spread over this rate, the analyst says. The 1.8% rate implies a 370-bp yield spread for the trust, higher than the average of 320 bps for the past four years. DBS raises the target price for the trust's units to S$1.08 from S$0.98, with an unchanged buy rating. Units are 0.5% lower at S$0.975. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - Bitcoin rises after the White House confirmed that President Trump would meet with Chinese leader Xi Jinping next week in South Korea. The two leaders will meet on October 30 on the sidelines of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation Summit. Trump said later Thursday that he thinks "we are going to come out very well and everyone's going to be very happy." The news raises hopes for a de-escalation in tensions. Trump's threat earlier this month of fresh tariffs on China sparked a selloff in cryptocurrencies. Bitcoin rises 1.6% to $111,390, LSEG data show. It has recovered from last Friday's near four-month low of $103,659 but remains well below the Oct.6 record high of $126,223. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0644 GMT - The dollar rises ahead of data at 1230 GMT that are expected to show U.S. inflation accelerated in September. Official data have been halted during the U.S. government shutdown. However, the Bureau of Labor Statistics called back some workers to release the inflation figures, albeit nine days late. Economists in a WSJ survey expect inflation to rise to 3.1% year-on-year in September from 2.9% in August. "We see risks skewed towards a higher reading but think the bar for the Federal Reserve skipping its planned rate cut on October 29 is very high," Danske Bank analysts say in a note. The U.S. purchasing managers' survey at 1345 GMT will also gain attention. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 99.036. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0629 GMT - The coming European data could challenge the minuscule trading range in German Bunds at the end of the week, Commerzbank Research's Christoph Rieger says in a note. In particular, flash estimate French, German and eurozone purchasing managers data are awaited on Friday morning, followed by further releases next week. Meanwhile, France lines up for a rating review by Moody's Ratings and Belgium will be reviewed by S&P Global Ratings. "The next downgrades of France and Belgium look in store tonight," the head of rates and credit research says. The 10-year Bund yield is up 0.6 basis points at 2.585%, the 10-year French OAT yield rises 0.3bps to 3.380% and the 10-year Belgian OLO yield rises 0.4bps to 3.137%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0625 GMT - Sterling falls against a stronger dollar but holds steady versus the euro, barely reacting even after data showed U.K. retail sales unexpectedly rose in September. Retail sales grew 0.5% compared to a month ago. Economists in a WSJ survey expected a 0.4% decline. It follows a 0.6% rise in August, revised from a 0.5% increase previously. It comes after Wednesday's lower-than-expected U.K. inflation data that strengthened the case for another Bank of England interest-rate cut by December and weakened sterling. Sterling falls 0.1% to $1.3314 compared with $1.3325 before Friday's data. The euro trades flat at 0.8713 pounds, little changed from levels before the data. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0617 GMT - Low volatility in eurozone rates markets is limiting the rebuilding of the term premium, but the process is continuing and driven by the supply-demand imbalance, say Societe Generale rates strategists in a note. Two- and 10-year eurozone government bond yields have been rangebound for months, while 30-year rates stabilized in the second half of 2025, they say. "This has led to a slowdown in the term premium on the euro curve," the strategists say. However, the potential for a higher term premium remains large, driven by risks of increased net government bond supply in 2026, continuing quantitative tightening, and the Dutch pension reform, they say. Term premium is a compensation to buy a longer-dated bond rather than a shorter-dated one. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0609 GMT - South Korea's economic expansion likely accelerated in 3Q on fiscal stimulus and resilient exports. The median forecast of 11 economists in a WSJ poll puts July-September GDP growth at 1.0% on quarter and 1.5% on year. That would be faster than the revised GDP growth of 0.7% on quarter and 0.6% on year in 2Q. "The anticipated uptick is primarily owing to government cash handouts," ING economist Min Joo Kang says in a note. Strong exports of semiconductors and vessels could continue, but their impact will likely be offset by weak shipments of other goods, Kang says. The 3Q GDP data are due on Tuesday. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0600 GMT - The Federal Reserve has turned more dovish amid labor market deterioration, but remains highly sensitive to fluctuations in inflation, says Nomura Capital Management's CIO Matthew Pallai in a note. He is watching how lingering price pressures from tariffs and immigration policy could complicate the disinflation trend. Stretched valuations in equities and corporate credit may heighten volatility if inflation surprises to the upside.(emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0553 GMT - A significant part, 72%, of U.S. CPI components are growing faster than the Federal Reserve's 2% inflation target, Aptus Capital Advisors' John Luke Tyner says in a note. Service inflation continues to linger above target, coupled with a risk for a delayed impact from tariffs, the head of fixed income says. Inflation has remained above the Fed's target for over 50 months, and it's expected to stay there until 2028, he says. In that case, deep Fed interest-rate cuts are unlikely, says Tyner, who is also portfolio manager. September CPI data is scheduled for release at 1430 GMT. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Gilt Yields Fall as U.S.-China Trade Deal Prospects Boost Sentiment — Market Talk
data.description: Yields on U.K. government bonds fall, along with their eurozone peers, as expectations of a trade deal between the U.S. and China ease investor concerns. Gilt yields also remain lower after below-forecast U.K. inflation data earlier this week. They show little reaction to U.K. retail sales data for September which was better than expected, indicating that consumer spending remains strong. The 10-year gilt yield falls 1.1 basis points to last trade at 4.404%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Inission Lowers Financial Targets For 2025
data.description:
Inission AB :

  • REVISES ITS FINANCIAL TARGETS FOR 2025

  • FULL-YEAR REVENUE TARGET FOR 2025 IS LOWERED FROM SEK 2.2 BILLION TO BETWEEN SEK 2.1-2.2 BILLION

  • EBITA MARGIN TARGET OF >6% IS REDUCED TO APPROXIMATELY 5%

  • REVISION IS EXPLAINED AS A DIRECT CONSEQUENCE OF DECRESED EARNINGS IN ENEDO (OEM) BUSINESS AREA

data.title: Holcim CEO seeing recovery in European construction market
data.description:
Holcim is seeing signs of recovery in the European construction market, Chief Executive Miljan Gutovic said on Friday, although the big impact from Germany's infrastructure spending is expected to come around the middle of next year.

"We are seeing good momentum, eastern Europe is really strong. I'm very pleased with the momentum we are seeing in Spain, Italy and Greece, which is driven by a combination of infrastructure, and the residential/commercial sector," said Gutovic after the building materials company reported its third quarter results.

"Countries like Germany, the UK and France have been soft in the past few years, but we are definitely seeing signs of a recovery," Gutovic told reporters.

data.title: Nomination Committee for Scandi Standard for the annual general meeting 2026
data.description:
Scandi Standard

The nomination committee for Scandi Standard AB's (publ) annual general meeting 2026 consists of the following members:

  • Avelino Gaspar (chairman, appointed by Lusiaves Group);
  • Anders Wennberg (appointed by Investment AB Öresund);
  • Henrik Sundell (appointed by Lantmännen);
  • Nicklas Paulson (appointed by Eva Qviberg); and
  • Johan Bygge (chairman of the board of directors of Scandi Standard).

The nomination committee has been appointed in accordance with the instructions for the nomination committee adopted by the annual general meeting 2020.

The nomination committee is to prepare and make proposals to the annual general meeting 2026 regarding:

  • elections of the chairman of the annual general meeting, the chairman of the board of directors and other members of the board of directors to be elected by the annual general meeting as well as the auditor;
  • resolutions on the remuneration to members of the board of directors elected by the annual general meeting that are not employees of the company as well as to the auditor; and
  • amendments to the instruction for appointing the members of the nomination committee if such are considered to be necessary.

Shareholders who wish to submit proposals to the nomination committee should preferably do so no later than 1 February 2026 so that the nomination committee has time to consider the proposals. Proposals may be submitted to the secretary of the Nomination Committee, Fredrik Olsson by email to fredrik.olsson@scandistandard.com or by post to Scandi Standard AB (publ), att. Fredrik Olsson, Box 30174, 104 25 Stockholm.

The nomination committee's proposals will be presented in the notice to attend the annual general meeting 2026 and on the company's website, www.scandistandard.com.

For further information, please contact:

Henrik Heiberg, Head of M&A, Financing & IR: +47 917 47 724

About Scandi Standard

Scandi Standard is the leading producer of chicken-based food products in the Nordic region and Ireland. The company produces, markets and sells ready to eat, chilled and frozen products under the well-known brands Kronfågel, Danpo, Den Stolte Hane, Manor Farm and Naapurin Maalaiskana. Eggs are also produced and sold in Norway. We are approximately 3,200 employees with annual sales of more than SEK 13 billion. For more information, please visit www.scandistandard.com.

https://news.cision.com/scandi-standard/r/nomination-committee-for-scandi-standard-for-the-annual-general-meeting-2026%2Cc4256132

Press release (PDF)

data.title: SNG: Jordan: National exports up 8% in first eight months of 2025 — DoS
data.description:
Staff Writer

AMMAN — Jordan’s exports rose by 8 per cent during the first eight months of 2025, while re-exports increased by 4.8 per cent, according to the Department of Statistics’ (DoS) monthly foreign trade report released on Thursday.

The overall value of exports grew by 7.7 per cent compared with the same period last year, the report showed. Imports also recorded a 5.6 per cent rise, resulting in a 3.5 per cent increase in the trade deficit compared with the corresponding period in 2024.

The coverage ratio of total exports to imports stood at 51 per cent during the January–August period, up from 50 per cent a year earlier. In August alone, the ratio remained unchanged at 50 per cent compared with August 2024.

According to the DoS, total exports during the first eight months reached JD6.71 billion, including JD6.098 billion in national exports and JD612 million in re-exports. Imports during the same period amounted to JD13.156 billion.

In August, total exports reached JD912 million, JD830 million in national exports and JD82 million in re-exports, while imports totalled JD1.837 billion.

Monthly data showed that total exports in August rose by 5.8 per cent compared with August 2024. National exports increased by 5.5 per cent, re-exports by 9.3 per cent, and imports by 7.1 per cent, according to the department.

data.title: Czechia Business Sentiment at Over 3-Year High
data.description:
The business confidence indicator in the Czech Republic increased by 1.8 percentage points 103.4 in October 2025, up from 101.6 in the preceding month.

This improvement was driven by a rise in sentiment within the industry sector, which saw a notable gain of 4.3 points to 99.8 from 95.5, and in selected services which rose by 0.6 points to 106.2.

Conversely, confidence weakened in other sectors including construction (121.8 vs 125.4) and trade (95.6 vs 99.6).

data.title: U.S. Futures, European Stocks Edge Up
data.description:
U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were up 0.2% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 0.1%. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

In Europe, the Stoxx Europe 600 gained 0.3% in morning trading. Lifco Series B rose 10% and Trelleborg Series B rose 7%. On the other hand, HIAB dropped 9.9%, and Signify slipped 7.4%. The FTSE 100 added 0.1%. Other stocks in Europe were up as France's CAC 40 increased 0.2% and Germany's DAX rose 0.2%.

The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index was flat at 96.28.

In commodities, Brent crude dropped 0.5% to $65.64 a barrel, and WTI crude fell 0.4% to $61.52 a barrel. The European benchmark price for natural gas, the Dutch futures contract TTF, was down 1% to 32.14 euros a megawatt hour.

The German 10-year Bund yield was flat at 2.589%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury held steady at 4.007%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.

Stocks in Asia were up as Japan's Nikkei 225 index climbed 1.4%, and Hong Kong's Hang Seng climbed 0.7%. China's benchmark Shanghai Composite climbed 0.7%.

-This article was automatically generated by MarketWatch.
